schema_json.description:
LLM contract for the agentic search loop 'Reason' step.

After each round of searches, the LLM analyzes results and decides
whether to continue searching or converge. Generates targeted
follow-up queries informed by what was found so far.

key_findings.description: 3-5 key findings from the latest round
key_findings.title: Key Findings

perspective_gaps.description: Underrepresented STORM perspectives in current results
perspective_gaps.title: Perspective Gaps

web_queries.description: 3-6 web queries for the next round
web_queries.title: Web Queries

academic_queries.description: 1-3 Semantic Scholar queries using precise terminology
academic_queries.title: Academic Queries

exa_queries.description: 0-1 semantic queries for Exa neural search
exa_queries.title: Exa Queries

should_continue.description: Whether another search round is needed
should_continue.title: Should Continue

reasoning.description: Brief rationale for the convergence assessment
reasoning.title: Reasoning

knowledge_gaps.description: Specific knowledge gaps identified from reading content
knowledge_gaps.title: Knowledge Gaps

schema_json.title: AgenticRoundAnalysis

prompt:
You are a research search strategist conducting an agentic deep research loop.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the current best-available estimates of permafrost carbon release under RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 scenarios by 2100?

ROUND HISTORY:
Round 1: 9 queries -> 80 web + 30 academic, 62 new URLs, 6 pages read

KNOWLEDGE ACQUIRED (6 pages read so far):
[Page 1] Permafrost and wildfire carbon emissions indicate need for ... - Nature
  Summary: Abstract Rapid Arctic warming is thawing carbon-rich permafrost, releasing greenhouse gases that accelerate climate change. Despite the importance of this feedback, permafrost-enabled global-scale models simulate only gradual, top-down thickening of the seasonally-thawed soil. This ignores abrupt permafrost thaw and intensifying fire regimes that combust soil carbon and further accelerate thaw.
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Our model suggests that including permafrost thaw and fire-related carbon emissions reduces the remaining allowable carbon budgets from 2025 onward by 25 % ± 12 % for avoiding 1.5 °C and 17 % ± 7 % for avoiding 2.0 °C, relative to simulations without; This ‘gradual thaw’ is the most widespread mechanism of permafrost degradation and is expected to result in the loss of 24–69% of near-surface permafrost by 210013,14,15,16,17.; Notable among these are thermokarst (hereafter used interchangeably with ‘abrupt thaw’) processes, in which the thawing of permafrost that contains excess ground ice results in uneven subsidence or even collapse of the ground surface and subsequent e
[Page 2] Continued permafrost ecosystem carbon loss under net-zero and ...
  Summary: INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT
  Perspectives: none tagged
  Key facts: none extracted
[Page 3] [PDF] Current and Future Permafrost Emissions as Large as Major Emitters
  Summary: It is actually a frozen mixture of soil, rocks, ice and organic material, holding about twice as much carbon as currently exists in Earth’s atmosphere. Cold temperatures have protected this organic matter from thawing, decomposing and releasing its stored carbon for many thousands of years. Observations confirm that it is rapidly warming and releasing part of that thawed carbon into the atmosphere as both carbon dioxide (CO 2) and methane (CH 4).
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Increasing climate-driven disturbances have weakened or reversed the long-term CO 2 sink in Boreal forest and Tundra biomes with inland waters and wetlands contributing high methane fluxes.; Today, at about 1.2°C, we are already committed to losing about 25% of surface permafrost.; As temperatures have risen, especially since about 1950, permafrost has not only declined in area but thawed to deeper depth and greater volume, beginning to release its stored carbon.
[Page 4] Unaccounted emissions from abrupt permafrost thaw and wildfires ...
  Summary: When accounting for carbon emissions from abrupt permafrost thaw and intensifying wildfire regimes, the remaining carbon budgets to keep global temperatures below 1.5°C were reduced by about one quarter and by nearly one fifth for 2°C. Abrupt thaw mainly occurs in ice-rich permafrost landscapes, where rapid thawing can cause the ground to collapse. This exposes deep carbon-rich soils to warmer temperatures, allowing large amounts of previously frozen carbon to be released over a short period of 
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: When accounting for carbon emissions from abrupt permafrost thaw and intensifying wildfire regimes, the remaining carbon budgets to keep global temperatures below 1.5°C were reduced by about one quarter and by nearly one fifth for 2°C.
[Page 5] Uncertainties in global permafrost area extent estimates from ...
  Summary: INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT
  Perspectives: none tagged
  Key facts: none extracted
[Page 6] Permafrost and the Global Carbon Cycle - NOAA Arctic
  Summary: Schuur Center for Ecosystem Science and Society, Northern Arizona University, Flagstaff, AZ, USA Highlights - Northern permafrost region soils contain 1,460-1,600 billion metric tons of organic carbon, about twice as much as currently contained in the atmosphere. - This pool of organic carbon is climate-sensitive. These observations signify that the feedback to accelerating climate change may already be underway. Introduction The Arctic continues to warm at a rate that is currently twice as fast
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Warming is causing perennially-frozen ground (permafrost) to thaw, with permafrost in many locations currently reaching record high temperatures (Biskaborn et al. 2019).; This permafrost carbon is the remnants of plants, animals, and microbes that have lived and died in tundra and boreal ecosystems, accumulating in frozen soil over hundreds to thousands of years (Schuur et al. 2008).; It updates material included in an essay on the terrestrial carbon cycle that appeared in Arctic Report Card 2016 (Schuur and Hugelius, 2016).

PERSPECTIVE COVERAGE:
  Covered (4/9): Scientific, Regulatory, Economic, Regional
  Uncovered: Industry, Public_Health, Historical, Methodological, Emerging_Trends
  Low coverage: Regulatory, Regional

Based on what has been READ:
1. Decompose the research question into 5-8 sub-questions.
   Which sub-questions have ZERO or WEAK evidence?
   Which sub-questions are well-answered with strong evidence?
2. What CONTRADICTIONS exist between sources? Flag conflicting evidence.
3. What specific KNOWLEDGE GAPS remain? List them as specific factual questions
   we CANNOT yet answer. 'none' is almost never correct for a complex research question.
4. Rate overall confidence in accumulated knowledge: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH with reasoning.
5. Which perspectives need more coverage?
6. Generate 3-6 targeted WEB queries to fill those gaps.
7. Generate 1-3 ACADEMIC queries using precise terminology.
8. Optionally generate 0-1 semantic EXA queries.
9. Assess ANSWER COMPLETENESS for the research question:
   - List specific sub-questions that remain UNANSWERED.
   - For each answered sub-question, rate evidence strength (weak/moderate/strong).
   - 'expanding': Major sub-questions still unanswered.
   - 'narrowing': Most sub-questions answered, seeking confirmation/depth.
   - 'saturated': All sub-questions answered with strong evidence.
10. Should we continue searching?

Focus on queries that address content-level gaps, not just missing URLs.
